text: PPH Recognition and First Response: CHW Emergency Card

PPH means 'postpartum haemorrhage'. This is very heavy bleeding after a baby is born. PPH is the number one cause of mothers dying after childbirth. It can happen very fast — a woman can go from normal to very sick in 30 minutes. Every CHW must know the warning signs and must act immediately.
What Is Heavy Bleeding After Birth?

After birth, every woman has some bleeding — this is normal. The normal blood comes in the first few days and slowly becomes less. But some women bleed too much, too fast. This is PPH and it is an emergency.
PPH most often happens in the first 24 hours after birth. But it can also happen up to 6 weeks later. Be alert for any heavy bleeding in this whole time.

Signs that the bleeding is too much: (1) She soaks more than 1 large cloth pad (or local equivalent) in 15 minutes. (2) She is passing big blood clots (lumps of blood). (3) Her belly does not feel firm and round like a ball — it feels soft. (4) She feels faint, dizzy, or like she is going to fall. (5) She is confused or not answering well. (6) Her skin is pale, cold, or sweaty.
⚠️ If you see ANY of these signs — THIS IS AN EMERGENCY. Get help now. Do not wait.

While someone goes to get a vehicle or health worker: (1) Ask the mother to lie down flat on her back. (2) If she is conscious, rub the top of her belly (the womb) firmly in a circular motion — you may feel it become firm like a ball. Keep doing this. (3) Have her put the baby to her breast — breastfeeding causes the womb to contract and slow the bleeding. (4) Keep her warm with a blanket. (5) Do not give food or water by mouth in case she needs surgery.
Do NOT: try to remove anything from the vagina, give herbal medicines, wait to see if it gets better, leave her alone.
Get Her to the Health Centre — NOW

⚠️ REFER IMMEDIATELY. PPH is the fastest-killing emergency in maternity care. Every minute matters. Use whatever transport is available — do not wait for the 'best' option. Go in a motorbike, a car, a cart, being carried — whatever is fastest.
Call the health centre ahead if you can so they are ready when you arrive. If your community has a referral system (vehicle fund, community ambulance), activate it NOW.
What to Tell the Health Worker

Tell the health worker: (1) She just had a baby — say when (today, yesterday, last week). (2) She has very heavy bleeding. (3) Tell them what you saw — how many pads soaked, any clots, whether she is conscious. (4) Tell them what first actions you took. (5) Any medicines she has taken.
⚠️ REMEMBER: You cannot treat PPH at home. Your job is to recognise it fast, do first actions to slow the bleeding, and get her to the health centre as fast as possible. Speed saves her life.
